differential_diagnoses: Portal biliopathy with associated portal hypertension and ectopic varices
Hemobilia secondary to hepaticojejunostomy stricture or pseudoaneurysm
Hepatocellular carcinoma or cholangiocarcinoma causing vascular invasion
Chronic portal vein thrombosis with cavernous transformation
Vascular ectasias or arteriovenous malformations in the biliary tree
next_steps: Hepatic angiography or CT angiography with focus on the biliary tree and hepatic vasculature
Endoscopic ultrasound (EUS) to evaluate perihilar region and vascular architecture
Interventional radiology consultation for evaluation of portal venous stenosis and potential portal pressure measurement
Surgical consultation regarding the prior hepaticojejunostomy site and biliary anatomy
Cross-sectional imaging follow-up or biopsy of the perihilar mass-like signal abnormality

differential_diagnoses: Hemobilia secondary to hepatic artery pseudoaneurysm or vascular-biliary fistula
Portal hypertensive biliopathy with biliary varices
Biliary-enteric fistula (choledochoenteric) at the hepaticojejunostomy site
Occult malignancy (e.g., hilar cholangiocarcinoma) with vascular invasion
Portal hypertension-associated small bowel varices or enteropathy
Chronic portal vein thrombosis with cavernous transformation and biliary congestion
next_steps: Perform catheter-directed angiography of the celiac and superior mesenteric arteries to evaluate for pseudoaneurysm and arteriovenous/biliary fistulae
Refer for double-balloon enteroscopy to visualize the hepaticojejunostomy site and look for bleeding source or ectopic varices
Consult interventional radiology for portal pressure measurements and potential portal vein intervention
Obtain endoscopic ultrasound (EUS) to characterize the perihilar mass-like lesion and evaluate the portal vasculature
Schedule elective surgical consultation for evaluation of the prior hepaticojejunostomy and potential biliary reconstruction
